explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & { }^n C_0+\frac{1}{2}{ }^n C_1+\frac{1}{3}{ }^n C_2+\ldots+\frac{1}{n+1}{ }^n C_n \\ & =\sum_{r=0}^n \frac{1}{r+1}{ }^n C_r \\ & =\sum_{r=0}^n \frac{1}{n+1}{ }^{n+1} C_{r+1} \quad \ldots\left[\because{ }^{n+1} C_{r+1}=\frac{n+1}{r+1}\left({ }^n C_r\right)\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{n+1} \sum_{r=0}^n{ }^{n+1} C_{r+1} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & =\frac{1}{n+1}\left(2^{n+1}-1\right) \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{2^{n+1}-1}{n+1}=\frac{1023}{10} \\ & \Rightarrow n=9 \end{array} $$

content: The value of $$\frac{{ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}}{{ }^{11} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}}$$, when both the numerator and denominator are at their greatest values, is
explanation:
Greatest value of $${ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}$$ is at $$\mathrm{r}=5$$

Greatest value of $${ }^{11} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}$$ is at $$\mathrm{r}=5$$

$$\therefore \quad \frac{{ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}}{{ }^{11} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}}=\frac{{ }^{10} \mathrm{C}_5}{{ }^{11} \mathrm{C}_5}=\frac{\frac{10 !}{5 ! 5 !}}{\frac{11 !}{5 ! 6 !}}=\frac{6}{11}$$

content: The difference between the maximum values of $${ }^6 C_r$$ and $${ }^n C_r$$ is 16, then $$n=$$
explanation:
The maximum value of $${ }^6 \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}$$ occurs at $$\mathrm{r}=\frac{6}{2}=3$$

$$\therefore{ }^6 \mathrm{C}_3=\frac{6!}{3!3!}=\frac{6 \times 5 \times 4}{6}=20$$

As per data given, $$\left|{ }^6 \mathrm{C}_3-{ }^{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_3\right|=16$$

$$\therefore{ }^n C_3=20+16 \text { or }{ }^n C_3=20-16$$

If $${ }^n C_3=36 \Rightarrow \frac{n!}{3!(n-3)!}=36 \Rightarrow n(n-1)(n-2)=216$$ is not possible for $$n \in N^{-}$$

If $${ }^n C_3=4 \Rightarrow \frac{n!}{(n-3)!3!}=4 \Rightarrow n(n-1)(n-2)=24$$

Here $$24=2 \times 3 \times 4 \Rightarrow n=4$$

$${ }^{\text {11}} \mathrm{C}_4+{ }^{11} \mathrm{C}_5+{ }^{12} \mathrm{C}_6+{ }^{13} \mathrm{C}_7={ }^{14} \mathrm{C}_7$$

We know that $${ }^n C_r+{ }^n C_{r-1}={ }^{n+1} C_r$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore{ }^{14} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{r}}=\left({ }^{11} \mathrm{C}_4+{ }^{11} \mathrm{C}_5\right)+{ }^{12} \mathrm{C}_6+{ }^{13} \mathrm{C}_7 \\ & =\left({ }^{12} \mathrm{C}_5+{ }^{12} \mathrm{C}_6\right)+{ }^{13} \mathrm{C}_7 \\ & ={ }^{13} \mathrm{C}_6+{ }^{13} \mathrm{C}_7={ }^{14} \mathrm{C}_7 \\ & \therefore r=7 \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: If the sum of the mean and the variance of a binomial distribution for 5 trials is 1.8 , then $p=$
explanation:
In Binomial distribution,

Mean $(\mu)=n p$

and variance $\left(\sigma^2\right)=n p q$

Given, $\mu+\sigma^2=1.8$

$$\begin{aligned} & n p+n p q=1.8 \\ & n p+n p(1-p)=1.8 \\ & 5 p+5 p-5 p^2=1.8 \quad[\because n=5] \\ & 5 p^2-10 p+1.8=0 \\ & 5 p^2-9 p-p+1.8=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 5 p(p-1.8)-1(p-1 \cdot 8)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(5 p-1)(p-1.8)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow p=0 \cdot 2(p \neq 1 \cdot 8) \quad(\because p \leq 1) \end{aligned}$$
